prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. For this household, target **9–12 months of essential expenses** in emergency savings. With one employed adult, four dependents, two young children, and homeownership—but no mortgage—the income concentration and potential childcare or home-repair costs justify more than the usual 3–6 months.

| Essential monthly expenses | 9-month fund | 12-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $27,000 | $36,000 |
| $4,000 | $36,000 | $48,000 |
| $5,000 | $45,000 | $60,000 |

**Formula:** monthly essentials × 9–12. Include property taxes, homeowners insurance, utilities, groceries, transportation, healthcare, childcare, minimum debt payments, and necessary home maintenance—not discretionary spending.

A sensible provisional target is **about $40,000–$50,000**, adjusted to the family’s actual essential spending. Keep it in an FDIC- or NCUA-insured high-yield savings or money-market deposit account—not stocks. Build it in stages: **$5,000 first, then 3 months, 6 months, and ultimately 9–12 months**.

Race and age do not materially determine this target; job stability, health coverage, debts, childcare needs, and the likelihood of the unemployed spouse returning to work do. If the employed spouse has unusually secure employment and the other spouse expects reliable work soon, **6–9 months** may be sufficient.

This is educational guidance, not individualized advice; no account data was provided.
